source_paper_title: Combinatorial Auctions with Interdependent Valuations: SOS to the Rescue
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Eden_2024, title={Combinatorial Auctions with Interdependent Valuations: SOS to the Rescue}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1371}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1371}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Eden, Alon and Feldman, Michal and Fiat, Amos and Goldner, Kira and Karlin, Anna R.}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={653–674} }
context:
Section 7 (problems), page 16 (in the provided PDF pagination)

For combinatorial auctions with multidimensional signals, is separability a necessary condition for achieving constant approximation to welfare? This problem is open even for single-dimensional signals and even for “simple” combinatorial valuations, such as unit demand.
title: Constant-factor welfare approximation without separability in multidimensional-signal combinatorial auctions
background_latex:
Consider a combinatorial auction with a set of agents  \( [n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a set of items \( [m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\). An allocation is a partition of items into disjoint bundles \((T_1,\dots,T_n)\) with \(T_i\subseteq [m]\) and \(T_i\cap T_{i'}=\emptyset\) for \(i\neq i'\); agent \(i\) receives bundle \(T_i\) (possibly \(\emptyset\)).

Signals are multidimensional over bundles: for every agent \(j\in[n]\) and every bundle \(T\subseteq [m]\), agent \(j\) has a private signal \(s_{jT}\in\mathbb{R}_+\). For each bundle \(T\subseteq[m]\), let \(s_T=(s_{1T},\dots,s_{nT})\in\mathbb{R}_+^n\). The full signal profile is \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\).

Valuations are interdependent: for each agent \(i\in[n]\) and bundle \(T\subseteq[m]\), the value of agent \(i\) for receiving bundle \(T\) is a known function \(v_{iT}(s_T)\in\mathbb{R}_+\) of the entire bundle-specific signal vector \(s_T\). Each \(v_{iT}\) is assumed (as in the paper’s model) to be weakly increasing in every coordinate of \(s_T\) and strictly increasing in the agent’s own signal coordinate \(s_{iT}\).

Incentives: a (possibly randomized) direct-revelation mechanism maps reported signals to an allocation and payments. The incentive notion of interest is universal ex post incentive compatibility with ex post individual rationality (universally ex post IC-IR): the mechanism is a distribution over deterministic mechanisms, each of which makes truthful reporting an ex post Nash equilibrium and guarantees nonnegative utility to truthful agents.

Objective: social welfare at signal profile \(s\) under allocation \((T_1,\dots,T_n)\) is \(\sum_{i=1}^n v_{iT_i}(s_{T_i})\). The optimal welfare is the maximum of this quantity over all feasible allocations.

Valuation structure: the paper focuses on the class of valuations that are submodular over signals (SOS). Informally, for each \(v_{iT}\), the marginal increase from raising a single coordinate \(s_{jT}\) is weakly larger when the other coordinates of \(s_T\) are smaller (a diminishing-returns property on \(\mathbb{R}_+^n\)). The paper obtains constant-factor welfare approximations in the multidimensional-signal model only under an additional structural assumption called separability, where \(v_{iT}(s_T)\) can be decomposed as a sum of a term depending only on other agents’ signals and a term depending only on \(i\)’s own signal.

The open question is whether such separability is actually required for constant-factor approximation in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial-auction model under SOS valuations.
problem_definition_latex: Determine whether there exists a universally ex post IC-IR mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) and a universal constant \(c\ge 1\) such that, for every instance of a combinatorial auction with multidimensional bundle-specific signals \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\) and SOS valuation functions \(\{v_{iT}:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}_+\}_{i\in[n],\,T\subseteq[m]}\) (with no separability assumption), the expected welfare achieved by \(\mathcal{M}\) at every signal profile \(s\) is at least \(\frac{1}{c}\) times the optimal welfare at \(s\).

Consider a combinatorial auction with a set of agents  \( [n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) and a set of items \( [m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\). Determine whether there exists a universally ex post IC-IR mechanism \(\mathcal{M}\) and a universal constant \(c\ge 1\) such that, for every instance of a combinatorial auction with multidimensional bundle-specific signals \(s=\{s_T\}_{T\subseteq[m]}\) and SOS valuation functions \(\{v_{iT}:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}_+\}_{i\in[n],\,T\subseteq[m]}\) (with no separability assumption), the expected welfare achieved by \(\mathcal{M}\) at every signal profile \(s\) is at least \(\frac{1}{c}\) times the optimal welfare at \(s\).
location_in_paper: Section 7 (problems), page 16 (in the provided PDF pagination)
open_problem_quote: For combinatorial auctions with multidimensional signals, is separability a necessary condition for achieving constant approximation to welfare? This problem is open even for single-dimensional signals and even for “simple” combinatorial valuations, such as unit demand.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks whether separability is necessary, so a counterexample would be a concrete SOS valuation family where every universally ex post IC-IR mechanism has super-constant (or unbounded) approximation ratio. Such hardness can often be explored by automated search over small instances (few agents/items, discrete signal grids) while encoding ex post IC constraints (e.g., via cycle-monotonicity) and optimizing the best achievable welfare ratio.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate hard instances (valuation tables over small discrete signal spaces satisfying SOS constraints) and compute the optimal achievable approximation ratio of mechanisms via linear/convex programs encoding ex post IC/IR feasibility, yielding an automatic fitness signal. Scaling to larger instances and ensuring SOS constraints may be computationally heavy, but iterative search could plausibly discover informative small counterexamples or suggest mechanism templates beyond separability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Eden–Feldman–Fiat–Goldner–Karlin, 2023) introduces the SOS condition (submodularity/diminishing returns over signals) as a substitute for single-crossing assumptions in interdependent-value auctions, and shows that random-partition mechanisms can be made universally ex post IC-IR while retaining a constant fraction of welfare when values are estimated using only a random subset of agents’ signals. In the multidimensional bundle-specific signal model, their constant-factor result (a 4-approximation via RS-VCG) relies crucially on separability of each bundle value into an “others’ signals” term plus an “own signal” term, so that VCG-style payments do not depend on the bidder’s report through the externality term.

Subsequent work has improved constants in more structured environments (e.g., binary signals and matroid constraints), indicating that SOS can indeed support strong welfare guarantees without single crossing in certain single-parameter settings. However, none of the available results remove the separability requirement in the fully multidimensional combinatorial-auction model of bundle-specific signals. Classical impossibility results for multidimensional interdependent settings (e.g., Jehiel–Moldovanu) continue to suggest that without additional structure, ex post implementability is severely constrained, and it remains unclear whether SOS alone suffices to recover constant-factor welfare.

Overall, the state of the art supports constant-factor welfare approximation under SOS in single-parameter settings and under separable-SOS in multidimensional combinatorial auctions, but the existence (or impossibility) of a universally ex post IC-IR constant-factor mechanism for general (nonseparable) SOS valuations with multidimensional bundle-specific signals remains open. Promising directions include identifying weaker payment-relevant decompositions than separability, alternative equilibrium notions, or impossibility constructions tailored to SOS that rule out constant factors in the nonseparable multidimensional model.

sources.title: Combinatorial Auctions with Interdependent Valuations: SOS to the Rescue
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces SOS (submodular-over-signals) valuations and gives universally ex post IC-IR welfare-approximation mechanisms: 4-approx for single-parameter downward-closed settings with single-dimensional signals (RS-V), 4-approx for combinatorial auctions with multidimensional signals under separable-SOS (RS-VCG), and (k+3)/O(log k) approximations for combinatorial auctions with single-dimensional signals and finite signal spaces.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether separability is necessary to obtain a constant-factor welfare approximation in combinatorial auctions with multidimensional bundle-specific signals under SOS valuations.

sources.title: Auctions with interdependence and SOS: Improved approximation
sources.theorem_or_result: Improves welfare approximation guarantees in SOS interdependent-value auctions for restricted settings (binary signals and matroid feasibility constraints), tightening the achievable factor toward the 1/2 lower bound in those cases.
sources.connection: Shows that in some structured environments constant factors can be improved without additional assumptions beyond SOS, but it does not address multidimensional bundle-specific signals nor remove separability in the combinatorial setting.

sources.title: Private interdependent valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies interdependent valuations under private-values-style informational assumptions and provides algorithmic/mechanism-design results and separations between SOS and single-crossing-type conditions in private settings.
sources.connection: Provides context on the role of SOS versus other conditions and highlights limitations when moving between public and private interdependence models; not a direct mechanism for multidimensional bundle-specific signals without separability.

sources.title: Instantiating the contingent bids model of truthful interdependent value auctions
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops contingent-bid auction formats for interdependent values and analyzes welfare guarantees in special combinatorial settings (e.g., single-minded bidders) with approximation factors depending on number of goods.
sources.connection: Represents earlier combinatorial-auction work with interdependent values but does not use SOS structure to obtain constant factors in the multidimensional bundle-specific signal model.

sources.title: Efficient design with interdependent valuations
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows broad impossibility results for efficient mechanism design with interdependent valuations and multidimensional signals (generic non-implementability), and characterizes special cases where efficiency is possible under strong structure (e.g., linearity and single-crossing-type conditions).
sources.connection: Motivates why Eden et al. pursue approximation and why additional structure (like separability) may be needed; provides barriers that any constant-factor approximation must circumvent in the multidimensional-signal combinatorial setting.
